26	
27	## Comprehensive Codebase Exploration Report
28	
29	### 1. Project Overview
30	**mailcow: dockerized** is a complete, open-source email server solution built on Docker and designed for self-hosting. It's a feature-rich mail server management platform that combines multiple specialized services into a cohesive dockerized environment.
31	
32	**Repository**: Fork of mailcow-dockerized (upstream: https://github.com/mailcow/mailcow-dockerized)
33	**Local Fork**: https://github.com/bonitobonita24/mail_lgucalapan_ph.git
34	**License**: GNU General Public License, Version 3
35	**Maintainer**: The Infrastructure Company GmbH (info@servercow.de)
36	**Latest Commit**: 8e9a9585 (Add SpecStory configuration files for project setup)
37	
38	---
39	
40	### 2. Key Directories and Their Roles
41	
42	| Directory | Purpose |
43	|-----------|---------|
44	| **`/data`** | Core application data, configuration, and web interface |
45	| **`/data/web`** | PHP-based web UI for mail server management (admin, user, domain admin interfaces) |
46	| **`/data/web/inc`** | Core PHP functions and includes (database access, authentication, mail operations) |
47	| **`/data/web/api`** | REST API with Swagger/OpenAPI documentation |
48	| **`/data/web/admin`** | Admin dashboard functionality (5 PHP files) |
49	| **`/data/Dockerfiles`** | Custom Docker images for each service (16 Dockerfiles) |
50	| **`/data/conf`** | Configuration files for all services (unbound, nginx, dovecot, postfix, rspamd, sogo, clamav, etc.) |
51	| **`/data/hooks`** | Custom hook scripts for service integration |
52	| **`/data/assets`** | Static assets (SSL certificates, templates) |
53	| **`.github/workflows`** | CI/CD automation (7 workflow files: image builds, PR checks, issue automation) |
54	| **`/_modules/scripts`** | Utility bash scripts for core functionality (4 core scripts) |
55	| **`/helper-scripts`** | Operational helpers (backup, restore, diagnostics, language updates) |
56	
57	---
58	
59	### 3. Technology Stack
60	
61	#### **Core Mail Services**
62	- **Postfix 3.7.11** - SMTP mail transport
63	- **Dovecot 2.3.21** - IMAP/POP3 with full-text search support
64	- **Postfix TLS Policy 1.8.23** - TLS policy enforcement
65	
66	#### **Backend Services**
67	- **PHP 8.2 (FPM)** - Web application runtime
68	- **MariaDB 10.11** - SQL database (mail data, configuration)
69	- **Redis 7.4.6** - Cache and session storage
70	- **Memcached** - Additional caching
71	- **Unbound 1.25** - DNS resolver (recursive)
72	
73	#### **Spam & Security**
74	- **Rspamd 3.14.3** - Advanced spam filtering (Bayesian, DKIM, SPF, DMARC)
75	- **ClamAV 1.71** - Antivirus scanning
76	- **Olefy 1.15** - OLE file scanning (Microsoft Office documents)
77	- **Netfilter** - Firewall isolation layer
78	
79	#### **Groupware & Webmail**
80	- **SOGo 5.12.5** - Unified webmail, calendar, contacts, and groupware
81	- **Nginx 1.06** - Reverse proxy and web server
82	
83	#### **Infrastructure**
84	- **Docker Compose** - Container orchestration (687 lines)
85	- **ACME/Let's Encrypt** - SSL certificate automation
86	- **Ofelia** - Scheduled job runner (cron replacement)
87	- **Watchdog** - Health monitoring and alerting
88	
89	#### **PHP Dependencies** (via Composer)
90	- **phpmailer** - Email handling
91	- **Twig 3.0** - Template engine
92	- **OAuth2 (bshaffer, league, keycloak)** - Authentication
93	- **ldaprecord** - LDAP directory integration
94	- **PHPMailParse** - MIME parsing
95	- **Bacon QR Code** - QR generation
96	- **2FA (RobThree/TwoFactorAuth)** - Two-factor authentication
97	- **Guzzle** - HTTP client
98	- **ADLdap2** - Active Directory integration
99	- **Carbon** - Date/time handling
```
</details></tool-use>

Fork of mailcow-dockerized (github.com/mailcow/mailcow-dockerized) customized for lgucalapan.ph mail hosting.

**Why:** Self-hosted email server for the lgucalapan.ph domain, running on a Komodo server.

The tech stack is PHP 8.2, MariaDB 10.11, Redis 7.4, Docker Compose with 18 services (Postfix, Dovecot, Rspamd, ClamAV, SOGo, Nginx, etc.). No package.json — pure PHP with Composer dependencies.

---
name: Deployment Workflow
description: Git push to GitHub > Komodo server pulls from GitHub > rebuilds containers. Never deploy directly.
type: project
---

Deployment pipeline: local dev -> push to GitHub (origin: bonitobonita24/mail_lgucalapan_ph) -> Komodo server pulls from GitHub -> rebuilds containers.

**Why:** User runs a Komodo server (https://komo.do/docs/intro) for container orchestration. All deployments go through GitHub as the source of truth. Komodo handles the pull-and-rebuild cycle.

**How to apply:**
- NEVER deploy directly to the server — always push to GitHub first
- Changes flow: local edit -> git push origin -> Komodo pulls -> container rebuild
- When making changes, commit and push to the GitHub repo; Komodo will handle the rest
- If asked to deploy, the correct action is `git push origin master` (or the working branch), not any direct server operation

```

---
name: Code Review PR 7180
description: First code review session on 2026-04-12 — reviewed rspamd SKIP_CLAMD fix, no issues above threshold
type: project
---

On 2026-04-12, reviewed PR #7180 "fix: disable default rspamd clamav integration when SKIP_CLAMD=y" using 5 parallel review agents.

Findings (all scored below 80 threshold, so not posted as issues):
- `rm -f` unconditionally deletes user's override.d/antivirus.conf on container start (score: 75)
- Grep guard checks local.d but custom external ClamAV configs could live in override.d (score: 75)
- Docker image tag not bumped alongside entrypoint changes (score: 50)
- Previous PR #6985 was blocked by maintainer @DerLinkman for same concern (score: 75, duplicate of guard issue)

Posted "No issues found" comment since no individual issue crossed the 80-confidence threshold.

**Why:** Establishes baseline for code review approach and scoring calibration.
**How to apply:** If reviewing similar rspamd/docker PRs in future, these edge cases are worth watching for.

```

I have all the information I need. Let me compile the comprehensive configuration report for the domain mail.lgucalapan.ph.

## Configuration Report for mail.lgucalapan.ph

Based on my exploration of `generate_config.sh` and `docker-compose.yml`, here are all required configuration variables for mailcow:

### CRITICAL REQUIRED VARIABLES (Must be set):

| Variable | Expected Value for mail.lgucalapan.ph | Description |
|----------|----------------------------------------|-------------|
| **MAILCOW_HOSTNAME** | mail.lgucalapan.ph | FQDN of the mail server (not a domain, but the server's hostname) |
| **MAILCOW_TZ** | Asia/Manila (or your timezone) | Server timezone (IANA format) |
| **DBNAME** | mailcow | MySQL database name |
| **DBUSER** | mailcow | MySQL database user |
| **DBPASS** | [auto-generated 28 chars] | MySQL password (auto-generated if not set) |
| **DBROOT** | [auto-generated 28 chars] | MySQL root password (auto-generated if not set) |
| **REDISPASS** | [auto-generated 28 chars] | Redis password (auto-generated if not set) |

### HTTP/HTTPS BINDINGS:

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| HTTP_PORT | 80 | HTTP port |
| HTTP_BIND | (empty) | HTTP bind address (leave empty for all interfaces) |
| HTTPS_PORT | 443 | HTTPS port |
| HTTPS_BIND | (empty) | HTTPS bind address (leave empty for all interfaces) |
| HTTP_REDIRECT | y | Redirect HTTP to HTTPS |

### EMAIL SERVICE PORTS:

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| SMTP_PORT | 25 | SMTP (unauthenticated mail submission) |
| SMTPS_PORT | 465 | SMTPS (implicit TLS) |
| SUBMISSION_PORT | 587 | Submission (authenticated client mail) |
| IMAP_PORT | 143 | IMAP (unencrypted) |
| IMAPS_PORT | 993 | IMAPS (TLS) |
| POP_PORT | 110 | POP3 (unencrypted) |
| POPS_PORT | 995 | POP3S (TLS) |
| SIEVE_PORT | 4190 | Sieve (mail filtering) |

### INTERNAL/MANAGEMENT PORTS:

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| DOVEADM_PORT | 127.0.0.1:19991 | Dovecot admin port (localhost only) |
| SQL_PORT | 127.0.0.1:13306 | MySQL port (localhost only) |
| REDIS_PORT | 127.0.0.1:7654 | Redis port (localhost only) |

### SSL/CERTIFICATE VARIABLES:

| Variable | Default | Notes |
|----------|---------|-------|
| SKIP_LETS_ENCRYPT | n | Enable Let's Encrypt ACME |
| ACME_DNS_CHALLENGE | n | Use DNS-01 challenge (requires DNS provider config) |
| ACME_DNS_PROVIDER | dns_xxx | DNS provider (if using DNS-01) |
| ACME_ACCOUNT_EMAIL | me@example.com | Let's Encrypt account email |
| ADDITIONAL_SAN | (empty) | Additional Subject Alternative Names (e.g., imap.*,smtp.*) |
| AUTODISCOVER_SAN | y | Generate certs for autodiscover.* and autoconfig.* |
| ADDITIONAL_SERVER_NAMES | (empty) | Alternative web UI server names |
| ENABLE_SSL_SNI | n | Use separate cert per domain (for >100 domains) |
| SKIP_IP_CHECK | n | Skip IPv4 check in ACME |
| SKIP_HTTP_VERIFICATION | n | Skip HTTP verification in ACME |

### DATABASE & CACHE:

| Variable | Default | Notes |
|----------|---------|-------|
| COMPOSE_PROJECT_NAME | mailcowdockerized | Docker Compose project name (lowercase) |
| IPV4_NETWORK | 172.22.1 | Internal Docker network (n.n.n format) |
| IPV6_NETWORK | fd4d:6169:6c63:6f77::/64 | Internal IPv6 network |
| SNAT_TO_SOURCE | (empty) | IPv4 SNAT for outgoing (optional) |
| SNAT6_TO_SOURCE | (empty) | IPv6 SNAT for outgoing (optional) |

### SECURITY & AUTHENTICATION:

| Variable | Default | Notes |
|----------|---------|-------|
| MAILCOW_PASS_SCHEME | BLF-CRYPT | Password hash algorithm |
| DOVECOT_MASTER_USER | (empty) | Dovecot master user (auto-gen if empty) |
| DOVECOT_MASTER_PASS | (empty) | Dovecot master pass (auto-gen if empty) |
| WEBAUTHN_ONLY_TRUSTED_VENDORS | n | Restrict WebAuthn to trusted vendors |
| ACL_ANYONE | disallow | Enable "anyone" pseudo-user ACL |
| ALLOW_ADMIN_EMAIL_LOGIN | n | Allow admins to login as email users in SOGo |

### SOGO (WEBMAIL/CALENDAR/CONTACTS):

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| SOGO_EXPIRE_SESSION | 480 | Session timeout in minutes |
| SOGO_URL_ENCRYPTION_KEY | (auto-gen 16 chars) | URL encryption key for SOGo |
| SKIP_SOGO | n | Disable SOGo (webmail, DAV, ActiveSync) |

### ADVANCED FILTERING & SCANNING:

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| SKIP_CLAMD | n | Disable ClamAV anti-virus |
| SKIP_OLEFY | n | Disable Olefy (Office doc scanning) |
| SKIP_FTS | n | Disable full-text search (Dovecot indexing) |
| SKIP_RSPAMD | n | Disable Rspamd spam filtering |
| FTS_HEAP | 128 | FTS heap size in MB |
| FTS_PROCS | 1 | Max FTS indexing processes |
| MAILDIR_GC_TIME | 7200 | Garbage collection time (minutes) |
| MAILDIR_SUB | Maildir | Maildir subdirectory name |
| SPAMHAUS_DQS_KEY | (empty) | Spamhaus DQS key (if on blocked ASN) |

### WATCHDOG MONITORING:

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| USE_WATCHDOG | y | Enable container health monitoring |
| WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_EMAIL | (empty) | Email addresses for alerts |
| WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_WEBHOOK | (empty) | Webhook URL for notifications |
| WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_WEBHOOK_BODY | (empty) | Custom webhook JSON body |
| WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_BAN | n | Notify on IP bans |
| WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_START | y | Notify when watchdog starts |
| WATCHDOG_EXTERNAL_CHECKS | n | Enable external relay checks |
| WATCHDOG_VERBOSE | n | Enable verbose logging |
| LOG_LINES | 9999 | Max log lines in Redis |

### API CONFIGURATION:

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| API_KEY | (empty) | Read-write API key |
| API_KEY_READ_ONLY | (empty) | Read-only API key |
| API_ALLOW_FROM | invalid | Comma-separated IPs allowed for API |

### MISC:

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| DOCKER_COMPOSE_VERSION | (auto-detected) | Docker Compose version (native/standalone) |
| ENABLE_IPV6 | true | Enable IPv6 support |
| SKIP_UNBOUND_HEALTHCHECK | n | Skip Unbound DNS health checks |
| DISABLE_NETFILTER_ISOLATION_RULE | n | Isolate Docker network |

---

### SETUP MECHANISM

The configuration uses a **`.env` symlink** that points to `mailcow.conf`:

```bash
ln -s mailcow.conf .env
```

This allows Docker Compose to automatically load all variables from `mailcow.conf` into the environment.

### MINIMAL WORKING CONFIGURATION FOR mail.lgucalapan.ph

To get mailcow running for this domain, you need to create `mailcow.conf` with at minimum:

```bash
MAILCOW_HOSTNAME=mail.lgucalapan.ph
MAILCOW_TZ=Asia/Manila
MAILCOW_PASS_SCHEME=BLF-CRYPT
DBNAME=mailcow
DBUSER=mailcow
DBPASS=[random 28+ alphanumeric chars]
DBROOT=[random 28+ alphanumeric chars]
REDISPASS=[random 28+ alphanumeric chars]
HTTP_PORT=80
HTTPS_PORT=443
HTTP_REDIRECT=y
SMTP_PORT=25
SMTPS_PORT=465
SUBMISSION_PORT=587
IMAP_PORT=143
IMAPS_PORT=993
TZ=Asia/Manila
COMPOSE_PROJECT_NAME=mailcowdockerized
ACL_ANYONE=disallow
MAILDIR_GC_TIME=7200
ADDITIONAL_SAN=
AUTODISCOVER_SAN=y
ADDITIONAL_SERVER_NAMES=
SKIP_LETS_ENCRYPT=n
ACME_DNS_CHALLENGE=n
ACME_ACCOUNT_EMAIL=admin@mail.lgucalapan.ph
ENABLE_SSL_SNI=n
SKIP_IP_CHECK=n
SKIP_HTTP_VERIFICATION=n
SKIP_CLAMD=n
SKIP_OLEFY=n
SKIP_SOGO=n
SKIP_FTS=n
FTS_HEAP=128
FTS_PROCS=1
ALLOW_ADMIN_EMAIL_LOGIN=n
USE_WATCHDOG=y
WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_BAN=n
WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_START=y
WATCHDOG_EXTERNAL_CHECKS=n
WATCHDOG_VERBOSE=n
LOG_LINES=9999
IPV4_NETWORK=172.22.1
IPV6_NETWORK=fd4d:6169:6c63:6f77::/64
SOGO_EXPIRE_SESSION=480
SOGO_URL_ENCRYPTION_KEY=[random 16 alphanumeric]
DOVECOT_MASTER_USER=
DOVECOT_MASTER_PASS=
WEBAUTHN_ONLY_TRUSTED_VENDORS=n
SPAMHAUS_DQS_KEY=
ENABLE_IPV6=true
DISABLE_NETFILTER_ISOLATION_RULE=n

1	#!/usr/bin/env bash
2	
3	# Ensure the script is run from the directory that contains a link of .env
4	# Resolve the directory this script lives in for consistent behavior when invoked from elsewhere
5	SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]:-$0}")" >/dev/null 2>&1 && pwd)"
6	
7	# Ensure script is executed in the mailcow installation directory by checking for a .env symlink that points to mailcow.conf
8	if [ ! -L "${PWD}/.env" ]; then
9	  echo -e "\e[33mPlease run this script from the mailcow installation directory.\e[0m"
10	  echo -e "  \e[36mcd /path/to/mailcow && ./generate_config.sh\e[0m"
11	  exit 1
12	fi
13	
14	# Verify the .env symlink points to a mailcow.conf file
15	env_target="$(readlink -f "${PWD}/.env" 2>/dev/null || true)"
16	if [ -z "$env_target" ] || [ "$(basename "$env_target")" != "mailcow.conf" ]; then
17	  echo -e "\e[31mThe found .env symlink does not point to a mailcow.conf file.\e[0m"
18	  echo -e "\e[33mPlease create a symbolic link .env -> mailcow.conf inside the mailcow directory and run this script there.\e[0m"
19	  echo -e "\e[33mNote: 'ln -s mailcow.conf .env' will create the symlink even if mailcow.conf does not yet exist.\e[0m"
20	  echo -e "  \e[36mcd /path/to/mailcow && ln -s mailcow.conf .env && ./generate_config.sh\e[0m"
21	  exit 1
22	fi
23	
24	# Load mailcow Generic Scripts
25	source _modules/scripts/core.sh
26	source _modules/scripts/ipv6_controller.sh
27	
28	set -o pipefail
29	
30	get_installed_tools
31	get_docker_version
32	
33	if [[ $docker_version -lt 24 ]]; then
34	  echo -e "\e[31mCannot find Docker with a Version higher or equals 24.0.0\e[0m"
35	  echo -e "\e[33mmailcow needs a newer Docker version to work properly...\e[0m"
36	  echo -e "\e[31mPlease update your Docker installation... exiting\e[0m"
37	  exit 1
38	fi
39	
40	detect_bad_asn
41	
42	### If generate_config.sh is started with --dev or -d it will not check out nightly or master branch and will keep on the current branch
43	if [[ ${1} == "--dev" || ${1} == "-d" ]]; then
44	  SKIP_BRANCH=y
45	else
46	  SKIP_BRANCH=n
47	fi
48	
49	if [ -f mailcow.conf ]; then
50	  read -r -p "A config file exists and will be overwritten, are you sure you want to continue? [y/N] " response
51	  case $response in
52	    [yY][eE][sS]|[yY])
53	      mv mailcow.conf mailcow.conf_backup
54	      chmod 600 mailcow.conf_backup
55	      ;;
56	    *)
57	      exit 1
58	    ;;
59	  esac
60	fi
61	
62	echo "Press enter to confirm the detected value '[value]' where applicable or enter a custom value."
63	while [ -z "${MAILCOW_HOSTNAME}" ]; do
64	  read -p "Mail server hostname (FQDN) - this is not your mail domain, but your mail servers hostname: " -e MAILCOW_HOSTNAME
65	  DOTS=${MAILCOW_HOSTNAME//[^.]};
66	  if [ ${#DOTS} -lt 1 ]; then
67	    echo -e "\e[31mMAILCOW_HOSTNAME (${MAILCOW_HOSTNAME}) is not a FQDN!\e[0m"
68	    sleep 1
69	    echo "Please change it to a FQDN and redeploy the stack with docker(-)compose up -d"
70	    exit 1
71	  elif [[ "${MAILCOW_HOSTNAME: -1}" == "." ]]; then
72	    echo "MAILCOW_HOSTNAME (${MAILCOW_HOSTNAME}) is ending with a dot. This is not a valid FQDN!"
73	    exit 1
74	  elif [ ${#DOTS} -eq 1 ]; then
75	    echo -e "\e[33mMAILCOW_HOSTNAME (${MAILCOW_HOSTNAME}) does not contain a Subdomain. This is not fully tested and may cause issues.\e[0m"

112	        ;;

115	      ;;

122	if [[ ${SKIP_BRANCH} != y ]]; then
123	  echo "Which branch of mailcow do you want to use?"
124	  echo ""
125	  echo "Available Branches:"
126	  echo "- master branch (stable updates) | default, recommended [1]"
127	  echo "- nightly branch (unstable updates, testing) | not-production ready [2]"
128	  echo "- legacy branch (supported until February 2026) | deprecated, security updates only [3]"
129	  sleep 1
130	
131	  while [ -z "${MAILCOW_BRANCH}" ]; do
132	    read -r -p  "Choose the Branch with it's number [1/2/3] " branch
133	    case $branch in
134	      [3])
135	        MAILCOW_BRANCH="legacy"
136	        ;;
137	      [2])
138	        MAILCOW_BRANCH="nightly"
139	        ;;
140	      *)
141	        MAILCOW_BRANCH="master"
142	      ;;
143	    esac
144	  done
145	
146	  git fetch --all
147	  git checkout -f "$MAILCOW_BRANCH"
148	
149	elif [[ ${SKIP_BRANCH} == y ]]; then
150	  echo -e "\033[33mEnabled Dev Mode.\033[0m"
151	  echo -e "\033[33mNot checking out a different branch!\033[0m"
152	  MAILCOW_BRANCH=$(git rev-parse --short $(git rev-parse @{upstream}))
153	
154	else
155	  echo -e "\033[31mCould not determine branch input..."
156	  echo -e "\033[31mExiting."
157	  exit 1
158	fi

161	  git_branch=${MAILCOW_BRANCH}
162	fi

168	cat << EOF > mailcow.conf
169	# ------------------------------
170	# mailcow web ui configuration
171	# ------------------------------
172	# example.org is _not_ a valid hostname, use a fqdn here.
173	# Default admin user is "admin"
174	# Default password is "moohoo"
175	MAILCOW_HOSTNAME=${MAILCOW_HOSTNAME}
176	
177	# Password hash algorithm
178	# Only certain password hash algorithm are supported. For a fully list of supported schemes,
179	# see https://docs.mailcow.email/models/model-passwd/
180	MAILCOW_PASS_SCHEME=BLF-CRYPT
181	
182	# ------------------------------
183	# SQL database configuration
184	# ------------------------------
185	DBNAME=mailcow
186	DBUSER=mailcow
187	
188	# Please use long, random alphanumeric strings (A-Za-z0-9)
189	DBPASS=${MAILCOW_DBPASS:-$(LC_ALL=C </dev/urandom tr -dc A-Za-z0-9 2> /dev/null | head -c 28)}
190	DBROOT=${MAILCOW_DBROOT:-$(LC_ALL=C </dev/urandom tr -dc A-Za-z0-9 2> /dev/null | head -c 28)}
191	
192	# ------------------------------
193	# REDIS configuration
194	# ------------------------------
195	REDISPASS=${MAILCOW_REDISPASS:-$(LC_ALL=C </dev/urandom tr -dc A-Za-z0-9 2> /dev/null | head -c 28)}
196	
197	# ------------------------------
198	# HTTP/S Bindings
199	# ------------------------------
200	
201	# You should use HTTPS, but in case of SSL offloaded reverse proxies:
202	# Might be important: This will also change the binding within the container.
203	# If you use a proxy within Docker, point it to the ports you set below.
204	# Do _not_ use IP:PORT in HTTP(S)_BIND or HTTP(S)_PORT
205	# IMPORTANT: Do not use port 8081, 9081, 9082 or 65510!
206	# Example: HTTP_BIND=1.2.3.4
207	# For IPv4 leave it as it is: HTTP_BIND= & HTTPS_PORT=
208	# For IPv6 see https://docs.mailcow.email/post_installation/firststeps-ip_bindings/
209	HTTP_PORT=80
210	HTTP_BIND=
211	
212	HTTPS_PORT=443
213	HTTPS_BIND=
214	
215	# Redirect HTTP connections to HTTPS - y/n
216	HTTP_REDIRECT=y
217	
218	# ------------------------------
219	# Other bindings
220	# ------------------------------
221	# You should leave that alone
222	# Format: 11.22.33.44:25 or 12.34.56.78:465 etc.
223	SMTP_PORT=25
224	SMTPS_PORT=465
225	SUBMISSION_PORT=587
226	IMAP_PORT=143
227	IMAPS_PORT=993
228	POP_PORT=110
229	POPS_PORT=995
230	SIEVE_PORT=4190
231	DOVEADM_PORT=127.0.0.1:19991
232	SQL_PORT=127.0.0.1:13306
233	REDIS_PORT=127.0.0.1:7654
234	
235	# Your timezone
236	# See https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_tz_database_time_zones for a list of timezones
237	# Use the column named 'TZ identifier' + pay attention for the column named 'Notes'
238	TZ=${MAILCOW_TZ}
239	
240	# Fixed project name
241	# Please use lowercase letters only
242	COMPOSE_PROJECT_NAME=mailcowdockerized
243	
244	# Used Docker Compose version
245	# Switch here between native (compose plugin) and standalone
246	# For more information take a look at the mailcow docs regarding the configuration options.
247	# Normally this should be untouched but if you decided to use either of those you can switch it manually here.
248	# Please be aware that at least one of those variants should be installed on your machine or mailcow will fail.
249	DOCKER_COMPOSE_VERSION=${COMPOSE_VERSION}
250	
251	# Set this to "allow" to enable the anyone pseudo user. Disabled by default.
252	# When enabled, ACL can be created, that apply to "All authenticated users"
253	# This should probably only be activated on mail hosts, that are used exclusively by one organisation.
254	# Otherwise a user might share data with too many other users.
255	ACL_ANYONE=disallow
256	
257	# Garbage collector cleanup
258	# Deleted domains and mailboxes are moved to /var/vmail/_garbage/timestamp_sanitizedstring
259	# How long should objects remain in the garbage until they are being deleted? (value in minutes)
260	# Check interval is hourly
261	MAILDIR_GC_TIME=7200
262	
263	# Additional SAN for the certificate
264	#
265	# You can use wildcard records to create specific names for every domain you add to mailcow.
266	# Example: Add domains "example.com" and "example.net" to mailcow, change ADDITIONAL_SAN to a value like:
267	#ADDITIONAL_SAN=imap.*,smtp.*
268	# This will expand the certificate to "imap.example.com", "smtp.example.com", "imap.example.net", "smtp.example.net"
269	# plus every domain you add in the future.
270	#
271	# You can also just add static names...
272	#ADDITIONAL_SAN=srv1.example.net
273	# ...or combine wildcard and static names:
274	#ADDITIONAL_SAN=imap.*,srv1.example.com
275	ADDITIONAL_SAN=
276	
277	# Obtain certificates for autodiscover.* and autoconfig.* domains.
278	# This can be useful to switch off in case you are in a scenario where a reverse proxy already handles those.
279	# There are mixed scenarios where ports 80,443 are occupied and you do not want to share certs
280	# between services. So acme-mailcow obtains for maildomains and all web-things get handled
281	# in the reverse proxy.
282	AUTODISCOVER_SAN=y
283	
284	# Additional server names for mailcow UI
285	#
286	# Specify alternative addresses for the mailcow UI to respond to
287	# This is useful when you set mail.* as ADDITIONAL_SAN and want to make sure mail.maildomain.com will always point to the mailcow UI.
288	# If the server name does not match a known site, Nginx decides by best-guess and may redirect users to the wrong web root.
289	# You can understand this as server_name directive in Nginx.
290	# Comma separated list without spaces! Example: ADDITIONAL_SERVER_NAMES=a.b.c,d.e.f
291	ADDITIONAL_SERVER_NAMES=
292	
293	# Skip running ACME (acme-mailcow, Let's Encrypt certs) - y/n
294	SKIP_LETS_ENCRYPT=n
295	
296	# Enable DNS-01 challenge for ACME (acme-mailcow) - y/n
297	# This requires you to set ACME_DNS_PROVIDER and ACME_ACCOUNT_EMAIL below
298	ACME_DNS_CHALLENGE=n
299	ACME_DNS_PROVIDER=dns_xxx
300	ACME_ACCOUNT_EMAIL=me@example.com
301	# You will need to pass provider-specific environment variables to the acme-mailcow container.
302	# See the dns-01 provider documentation for more information.
303	# for example for Azure DNS:
304	#AZUREDNS_SUBSCRIPTIONID=xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx
305	#AZUREDNS_TENANTID=xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx
306	#AZUREDNS_APPID=xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx
307	#AZUREDNS_CLIENTSECRET=xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx
308	#AZUREDNS_RESOURCEGROUP="your-resource-group"
309	#AZUREDNS_ZONE="your-zone-name"
310	
311	# Create separate certificates for all domains - y/n
312	# this will allow adding more than 100 domains, but some email clients will not be able to connect with alternative hostnames
313	# see https://doc.dovecot.org/admin_manual/ssl/sni_support
314	ENABLE_SSL_SNI=n
315	
316	# Skip IPv4 check in ACME container - y/n
317	SKIP_IP_CHECK=n
318	
319	# Skip HTTP verification in ACME container - y/n
320	SKIP_HTTP_VERIFICATION=n
321	
322	# Skip Unbound (DNS Resolver) Healthchecks (NOT Recommended!) - y/n
323	SKIP_UNBOUND_HEALTHCHECK=n
324	
325	# Skip ClamAV (clamd-mailcow) anti-virus (Rspamd will auto-detect a missing ClamAV container) - y/n
326	SKIP_CLAMD=${SKIP_CLAMD}
327	
328	# Skip Olefy (olefy-mailcow) anti-virus for Office documents (Rspamd will auto-detect a missing Olefy container) - y/n
329	SKIP_OLEFY=n
330	
331	# Skip SOGo: Will disable SOGo integration and therefore webmail, DAV protocols and ActiveSync support (experimental, unsupported, not fully implemented) - y/n
332	SKIP_SOGO=n
333	
334	# Skip FTS (Fulltext Search) for Dovecot on low-memory, low-threaded systems or if you simply want to disable it.
335	# Dovecot inside mailcow use Flatcurve as FTS Backend.
336	SKIP_FTS=n
337	
338	# Dovecot Indexing (FTS) Process maximum heap size in MB, there is no recommendation, please see Dovecot docs.
339	# Flatcurve (Xapian backend) is used as the FTS Indexer. It is supposed to be efficient in CPU and RAM consumption.
340	# However: Please always monitor your Resource consumption!
341	FTS_HEAP=128
342	
343	# Controls how many processes the Dovecot indexing process can spawn at max.
344	# Too many indexing processes can use a lot of CPU and Disk I/O.
345	# Please visit: https://doc.dovecot.org/configuration_manual/service_configuration/#indexer-worker for more information
346	FTS_PROCS=1
347	
348	# Allow admins to log into SOGo as email user (without any password)
349	ALLOW_ADMIN_EMAIL_LOGIN=n
350	
351	# Enable watchdog (watchdog-mailcow) to restart unhealthy containers
352	USE_WATCHDOG=y
353	
354	# Send watchdog notifications by mail (sent from watchdog@MAILCOW_HOSTNAME)
355	# CAUTION:
356	# 1. You should use external recipients
357	# 2. Mails are sent unsigned (no DKIM)
358	# 3. If you use DMARC, create a separate DMARC policy ("v=DMARC1; p=none;" in _dmarc.MAILCOW_HOSTNAME)
359	# Multiple rcpts allowed, NO quotation marks, NO spaces
360	#WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_EMAIL=a@example.com,b@example.com,c@example.com
361	#WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_EMAIL=
362	
363	# Send notifications to a webhook URL that receives a POST request with the content type "application/json".
364	# You can use this to send notifications to services like Discord, Slack and others.
365	#WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_WEBHOOK=https://discord.com/api/webhooks/XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX/XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
366	# JSON body included in the webhook POST request. Needs to be in single quotes.

370	# Notify about banned IP (includes whois lookup)

399	
400	# Use this IPv4 for outgoing connections (SNAT)

402	
403	# Use this IPv6 for outgoing connections (SNAT)

405	
406	# Create or override an API key for the web UI
407	# You _must_ define API_ALLOW_FROM, which is a comma separated list of IPs

412	
413	#API_KEY=
414	#API_KEY_READ_ONLY=
415	#API_ALLOW_FROM=172.22.1.1,127.0.0.1
416	
417	# mail_home is ~/Maildir
418	MAILDIR_SUB=Maildir
419	
420	# SOGo session timeout in minutes
421	SOGO_EXPIRE_SESSION=480
422	
423	# SOGo URL encryption key (exactly 16 characters, limited to A–Z, a–z, 0–9)
424	# This key is used to encrypt email addresses within SOGo URLs
425	SOGO_URL_ENCRYPTION_KEY=$(LC_ALL=C </dev/urandom tr -dc A-Za-z0-9 2>/dev/null | head -c 16)
426	
427	# DOVECOT_MASTER_USER and DOVECOT_MASTER_PASS must both be provided. No special chars.
428	# Empty by default to auto-generate master user and password on start.
429	# User expands to DOVECOT_MASTER_USER@mailcow.local
430	# LEAVE EMPTY IF UNSURE
431	DOVECOT_MASTER_USER=
432	# LEAVE EMPTY IF UNSURE

435	# WebAuthn device manufacturer verification

443	# If empty, it will completely disable Spamhaus blocklists if it detects that you are running on a server using a blocked AS.
444	# Otherwise it will work normally.

447	# IPv6 Controller Section
448	# This variable controls the usage of IPv6 within mailcow.
449	# Can either be true or false | Defaults to true
450	# WARNING: MAKE SURE TO PROPERLY CONFIGURE IPv6 ON YOUR HOST FIRST BEFORE ENABLING THIS AS FAULTY CONFIGURATIONS CAN LEAD TO OPEN RELAYS!

```text
cat << EOF > mailcow.conf
# ------------------------------
# mailcow web ui configuration
# ------------------------------
# example.org is _not_ a valid hostname, use a fqdn here.
# Default admin user is "admin"
# Default password is "moohoo"
MAILCOW_HOSTNAME=${MAILCOW_HOSTNAME}

# Password hash algorithm
# Only certain password hash algorithm are supported. For a fully list of supported schemes,
# see https://docs.mailcow.email/models/model-passwd/
MAILCOW_PASS_SCHEME=BLF-CRYPT

# ------------------------------
# SQL database configuration
# ------------------------------
DBNAME=mailcow
DBUSER=mailcow

# Please use long, random alphanumeric strings (A-Za-z0-9)
DBPASS=${MAILCOW_DBPASS:-$(LC_ALL=C </dev/urandom tr -dc A-Za-z0-9 2> /dev/null | head -c 28)}
DBROOT=${MAILCOW_DBROOT:-$(LC_ALL=C </dev/urandom tr -dc A-Za-z0-9 2> /dev/null | head -c 28)}

# ------------------------------
# REDIS configuration
# ------------------------------
REDISPASS=${MAILCOW_REDISPASS:-$(LC_ALL=C </dev/urandom tr -dc A-Za-z0-9 2> /dev/null | head -c 28)}

# ------------------------------
# HTTP/S Bindings
# ------------------------------

# You should use HTTPS, but in case of SSL offloaded reverse proxies:
# Might be important: This will also change the binding within the container.
# If you use a proxy within Docker, point it to the ports you set below.
# Do _not_ use IP:PORT in HTTP(S)_BIND or HTTP(S)_PORT
# IMPORTANT: Do not use port 8081, 9081, 9082 or 65510!
# Example: HTTP_BIND=1.2.3.4
# For IPv4 leave it as it is: HTTP_BIND= & HTTPS_PORT=
# For IPv6 see https://docs.mailcow.email/post_installation/firststeps-ip_bindings/
HTTP_PORT=80
HTTP_BIND=

HTTPS_PORT=443
HTTPS_BIND=

# Redirect HTTP connections to HTTPS - y/n
HTTP_REDIRECT=y

# ------------------------------
# Other bindings
# ------------------------------
# You should leave that alone
# Format: 11.22.33.44:25 or 12.34.56.78:465 etc.
SMTP_PORT=25
SMTPS_PORT=465
SUBMISSION_PORT=587
IMAP_PORT=143
IMAPS_PORT=993
POP_PORT=110
POPS_PORT=995
SIEVE_PORT=4190
DOVEADM_PORT=127.0.0.1:19991
SQL_PORT=127.0.0.1:13306
REDIS_PORT=127.0.0.1:7654

# Your timezone
# See https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_tz_database_time_zones for a list of timezones
# Use the column named 'TZ identifier' + pay attention for the column named 'Notes'
TZ=${MAILCOW_TZ}

# Fixed project name
# Please use lowercase letters only
COMPOSE_PROJECT_NAME=mailcowdockerized

# Used Docker Compose version
# Switch here between native (compose plugin) and standalone
# For more information take a look at the mailcow docs regarding the configuration options.
# Normally this should be untouched but if you decided to use either of those you can switch it manually here.
# Please be aware that at least one of those variants should be installed on your machine or mailcow will fail.
DOCKER_COMPOSE_VERSION=${COMPOSE_VERSION}

# Set this to "allow" to enable the anyone pseudo user. Disabled by default.
# When enabled, ACL can be created, that apply to "All authenticated users"
# This should probably only be activated on mail hosts, that are used exclusively by one organisation.
# Otherwise a user might share data with too many other users.
ACL_ANYONE=disallow

# Garbage collector cleanup
# Deleted domains and mailboxes are moved to /var/vmail/_garbage/timestamp_sanitizedstring
# How long should objects remain in the garbage until they are being deleted? (value in minutes)
# Check interval is hourly
MAILDIR_GC_TIME=7200

# Additional SAN for the certificate
#
# You can use wildcard records to create specific names for every domain you add to mailcow.
# Example: Add domains "example.com" and "example.net" to mailcow, change ADDITIONAL_SAN to a value like:
#ADDITIONAL_SAN=imap.*,smtp.*
# This will expand the certificate to "imap.example.com", "smtp.example.com", "imap.example.net", "smtp.example.net"
# plus every domain you add in the future.
#
# You can also just add static names...
#ADDITIONAL_SAN=srv1.example.net
# ...or combine wildcard and static names:
#ADDITIONAL_SAN=imap.*,srv1.example.com
ADDITIONAL_SAN=

# Obtain certificates for autodiscover.* and autoconfig.* domains.
# This can be useful to switch off in case you are in a scenario where a reverse proxy already handles those.
# There are mixed scenarios where ports 80,443 are occupied and you do not want to share certs
# between services. So acme-mailcow obtains for maildomains and all web-things get handled
```

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 2026-04-12 15:27:32Z)**_

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 2026-04-12 15:27:33Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="Bash"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Bash**</summary>

`sed -n '275,457p' /home/me/UbuntuDevFiles/lgucalapan_ph/mail_lgucalapan_ph/generate_config.sh`

```text
ADDITIONAL_SAN=

# Obtain certificates for autodiscover.* and autoconfig.* domains.
# This can be useful to switch off in case you are in a scenario where a reverse proxy already handles those.
# There are mixed scenarios where ports 80,443 are occupied and you do not want to share certs
# between services. So acme-mailcow obtains for maildomains and all web-things get handled
# in the reverse proxy.
AUTODISCOVER_SAN=y

# Additional server names for mailcow UI
#
# Specify alternative addresses for the mailcow UI to respond to
# This is useful when you set mail.* as ADDITIONAL_SAN and want to make sure mail.maildomain.com will always point to the mailcow UI.
# If the server name does not match a known site, Nginx decides by best-guess and may redirect users to the wrong web root.
# You can understand this as server_name directive in Nginx.
# Comma separated list without spaces! Example: ADDITIONAL_SERVER_NAMES=a.b.c,d.e.f
ADDITIONAL_SERVER_NAMES=

# Skip running ACME (acme-mailcow, Let's Encrypt certs) - y/n
SKIP_LETS_ENCRYPT=n

# Enable DNS-01 challenge for ACME (acme-mailcow) - y/n
# This requires you to set ACME_DNS_PROVIDER and ACME_ACCOUNT_EMAIL below
ACME_DNS_CHALLENGE=n
ACME_DNS_PROVIDER=dns_xxx
ACME_ACCOUNT_EMAIL=me@example.com
# You will need to pass provider-specific environment variables to the acme-mailcow container.
# See the dns-01 provider documentation for more information.
# for example for Azure DNS:
#AZUREDNS_SUBSCRIPTIONID=xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx
#AZUREDNS_TENANTID=xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx
#AZUREDNS_APPID=xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx
#AZUREDNS_CLIENTSECRET=xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx
#AZUREDNS_RESOURCEGROUP="your-resource-group"
#AZUREDNS_ZONE="your-zone-name"

# Create separate certificates for all domains - y/n
# this will allow adding more than 100 domains, but some email clients will not be able to connect with alternative hostnames
# see https://doc.dovecot.org/admin_manual/ssl/sni_support
ENABLE_SSL_SNI=n

# Skip IPv4 check in ACME container - y/n
SKIP_IP_CHECK=n

# Skip HTTP verification in ACME container - y/n
SKIP_HTTP_VERIFICATION=n

# Skip Unbound (DNS Resolver) Healthchecks (NOT Recommended!) - y/n
SKIP_UNBOUND_HEALTHCHECK=n

# Skip ClamAV (clamd-mailcow) anti-virus (Rspamd will auto-detect a missing ClamAV container) - y/n
SKIP_CLAMD=${SKIP_CLAMD}

# Skip Olefy (olefy-mailcow) anti-virus for Office documents (Rspamd will auto-detect a missing Olefy container) - y/n
SKIP_OLEFY=n

# Skip SOGo: Will disable SOGo integration and therefore webmail, DAV protocols and ActiveSync support (experimental, unsupported, not fully implemented) - y/n
SKIP_SOGO=n

# Skip FTS (Fulltext Search) for Dovecot on low-memory, low-threaded systems or if you simply want to disable it.
# Dovecot inside mailcow use Flatcurve as FTS Backend.
SKIP_FTS=n

# Dovecot Indexing (FTS) Process maximum heap size in MB, there is no recommendation, please see Dovecot docs.
# Flatcurve (Xapian backend) is used as the FTS Indexer. It is supposed to be efficient in CPU and RAM consumption.
# However: Please always monitor your Resource consumption!
FTS_HEAP=128

# Controls how many processes the Dovecot indexing process can spawn at max.
# Too many indexing processes can use a lot of CPU and Disk I/O.
# Please visit: https://doc.dovecot.org/configuration_manual/service_configuration/#indexer-worker for more information
FTS_PROCS=1

# Allow admins to log into SOGo as email user (without any password)
ALLOW_ADMIN_EMAIL_LOGIN=n

# Enable watchdog (watchdog-mailcow) to restart unhealthy containers
USE_WATCHDOG=y

# Send watchdog notifications by mail (sent from watchdog@MAILCOW_HOSTNAME)
# CAUTION:
# 1. You should use external recipients
# 2. Mails are sent unsigned (no DKIM)
# 3. If you use DMARC, create a separate DMARC policy ("v=DMARC1; p=none;" in _dmarc.MAILCOW_HOSTNAME)
# Multiple rcpts allowed, NO quotation marks, NO spaces
#WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_EMAIL=a@example.com,b@example.com,c@example.com
#WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_EMAIL=

# Send notifications to a webhook URL that receives a POST request with the content type "application/json".
# You can use this to send notifications to services like Discord, Slack and others.
#WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_WEBHOOK=https://discord.com/api/webhooks/XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX/XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
# JSON body included in the webhook POST request. Needs to be in single quotes.
# Following variables are available: SUBJECT, BODY
#WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_WEBHOOK_BODY='{"username": "mailcow Watchdog", "content": "**${SUBJECT}**\n${BODY}"}'

# Notify about banned IP (includes whois lookup)
WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_BAN=n

# Send a notification when the watchdog is started.
WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_START=y

# Subject for watchdog mails. Defaults to "Watchdog ALERT" followed by the error message.
#WATCHDOG_SUBJECT=

# Checks if mailcow is an open relay. Requires a SAL. More checks will follow.
# https://www.servercow.de/mailcow?lang=en
# https://www.servercow.de/mailcow?lang=de
# No data is collected. Opt-in and anonymous.
# Will only work with unmodified mailcow setups.
WATCHDOG_EXTERNAL_CHECKS=n

# Enable watchdog verbose logging
WATCHDOG_VERBOSE=n

# Max log lines per service to keep in Redis logs
LOG_LINES=9999

# Internal IPv4 /24 subnet, format n.n.n (expands to n.n.n.0/24)
# Use private IPv4 addresses only, see https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Private_network#Private_IPv4_addresses
IPV4_NETWORK=172.22.1

# Internal IPv6 subnet in fc00::/7
# Use private IPv6 addresses only, see https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Private_network#Private_IPv6_addresses
IPV6_NETWORK=fd4d:6169:6c63:6f77::/64

# Use this IPv4 for outgoing connections (SNAT)
#SNAT_TO_SOURCE=

# Use this IPv6 for outgoing connections (SNAT)
#SNAT6_TO_SOURCE=

# Create or override an API key for the web UI
# You _must_ define API_ALLOW_FROM, which is a comma separated list of IPs
# An API key defined as API_KEY has read-write access
# An API key defined as API_KEY_READ_ONLY has read-only access
# Allowed chars for API_KEY and API_KEY_READ_ONLY: a-z, A-Z, 0-9, -
# You can define API_KEY and/or API_KEY_READ_ONLY

#API_KEY=
#API_KEY_READ_ONLY=
#API_ALLOW_FROM=172.22.1.1,127.0.0.1

# mail_home is ~/Maildir
MAILDIR_SUB=Maildir

# SOGo session timeout in minutes
SOGO_EXPIRE_SESSION=480

# SOGo URL encryption key (exactly 16 characters, limited to A–Z, a–z, 0–9)
# This key is used to encrypt email addresses within SOGo URLs
SOGO_URL_ENCRYPTION_KEY=$(LC_ALL=C </dev/urandom tr -dc A-Za-z0-9 2>/dev/null | head -c 16)

# DOVECOT_MASTER_USER and DOVECOT_MASTER_PASS must both be provided. No special chars.
# Empty by default to auto-generate master user and password on start.
# User expands to DOVECOT_MASTER_USER@mailcow.local
# LEAVE EMPTY IF UNSURE
DOVECOT_MASTER_USER=
# LEAVE EMPTY IF UNSURE
DOVECOT_MASTER_PASS=

# WebAuthn device manufacturer verification
# After setting WEBAUTHN_ONLY_TRUSTED_VENDORS=y only devices from trusted manufacturers are allowed
# root certificates can be placed for validation under mailcow-dockerized/data/web/inc/lib/WebAuthn/rootCertificates
WEBAUTHN_ONLY_TRUSTED_VENDORS=n

# Spamhaus Data Query Service Key
# Optional: Leave empty for none
# Enter your key here if you are using a blocked ASN (OVH, AWS, Cloudflare e.g) for the unregistered Spamhaus Blocklist.
# If empty, it will completely disable Spamhaus blocklists if it detects that you are running on a server using a blocked AS.
# Otherwise it will work normally.
SPAMHAUS_DQS_KEY=

# IPv6 Controller Section
# This variable controls the usage of IPv6 within mailcow.
# Can either be true or false | Defaults to true
# WARNING: MAKE SURE TO PROPERLY CONFIGURE IPv6 ON YOUR HOST FIRST BEFORE ENABLING THIS AS FAULTY CONFIGURATIONS CAN LEAD TO OPEN RELAYS!
# A COMPLETE DOCKER STACK REBUILD (compose down && compose up -d) IS NEEDED TO APPLY THIS.
ENABLE_IPV6=${IPV6_BOOL}

# Prevent netfilter from setting an iptables/nftables rule to isolate the mailcow docker network - y/n
# CAUTION: Disabling this may expose container ports to other neighbors on the same subnet, even if the ports are bound to localhost
DISABLE_NETFILTER_ISOLATION_RULE=n
EOF
```

## Configuration Report for mail.lgucalapan.ph

Based on my exploration of `generate_config.sh` and `docker-compose.yml`, here are all required configuration variables for mailcow:

### CRITICAL REQUIRED VARIABLES (Must be set):

| Variable | Expected Value for mail.lgucalapan.ph | Description |
|----------|----------------------------------------|-------------|
| **MAILCOW_HOSTNAME** | mail.lgucalapan.ph | FQDN of the mail server (not a domain, but the server's hostname) |
| **MAILCOW_TZ** | Asia/Manila (or your timezone) | Server timezone (IANA format) |
| **DBNAME** | mailcow | MySQL database name |
| **DBUSER** | mailcow | MySQL database user |
| **DBPASS** | [auto-generated 28 chars] | MySQL password (auto-generated if not set) |
| **DBROOT** | [auto-generated 28 chars] | MySQL root password (auto-generated if not set) |
| **REDISPASS** | [auto-generated 28 chars] | Redis password (auto-generated if not set) |

### HTTP/HTTPS BINDINGS:

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| HTTP_PORT | 80 | HTTP port |
| HTTP_BIND | (empty) | HTTP bind address (leave empty for all interfaces) |
| HTTPS_PORT | 443 | HTTPS port |
| HTTPS_BIND | (empty) | HTTPS bind address (leave empty for all interfaces) |
| HTTP_REDIRECT | y | Redirect HTTP to HTTPS |

### EMAIL SERVICE PORTS:

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| SMTP_PORT | 25 | SMTP (unauthenticated mail submission) |
| SMTPS_PORT | 465 | SMTPS (implicit TLS) |
| SUBMISSION_PORT | 587 | Submission (authenticated client mail) |
| IMAP_PORT | 143 | IMAP (unencrypted) |
| IMAPS_PORT | 993 | IMAPS (TLS) |
| POP_PORT | 110 | POP3 (unencrypted) |
| POPS_PORT | 995 | POP3S (TLS) |
| SIEVE_PORT | 4190 | Sieve (mail filtering) |

### INTERNAL/MANAGEMENT PORTS:

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| DOVEADM_PORT | 127.0.0.1:19991 | Dovecot admin port (localhost only) |
| SQL_PORT | 127.0.0.1:13306 | MySQL port (localhost only) |
| REDIS_PORT | 127.0.0.1:7654 | Redis port (localhost only) |

### SSL/CERTIFICATE VARIABLES:

| Variable | Default | Notes |
|----------|---------|-------|
| SKIP_LETS_ENCRYPT | n | Enable Let's Encrypt ACME |
| ACME_DNS_CHALLENGE | n | Use DNS-01 challenge (requires DNS provider config) |
| ACME_DNS_PROVIDER | dns_xxx | DNS provider (if using DNS-01) |
| ACME_ACCOUNT_EMAIL | me@example.com | Let's Encrypt account email |
| ADDITIONAL_SAN | (empty) | Additional Subject Alternative Names (e.g., imap.*,smtp.*) |
| AUTODISCOVER_SAN | y | Generate certs for autodiscover.* and autoconfig.* |
| ADDITIONAL_SERVER_NAMES | (empty) | Alternative web UI server names |
| ENABLE_SSL_SNI | n | Use separate cert per domain (for >100 domains) |
| SKIP_IP_CHECK | n | Skip IPv4 check in ACME |
| SKIP_HTTP_VERIFICATION | n | Skip HTTP verification in ACME |

### DATABASE & CACHE:

| Variable | Default | Notes |
|----------|---------|-------|
| COMPOSE_PROJECT_NAME | mailcowdockerized | Docker Compose project name (lowercase) |
| IPV4_NETWORK | 172.22.1 | Internal Docker network (n.n.n format) |
| IPV6_NETWORK | fd4d:6169:6c63:6f77::/64 | Internal IPv6 network |
| SNAT_TO_SOURCE | (empty) | IPv4 SNAT for outgoing (optional) |
| SNAT6_TO_SOURCE | (empty) | IPv6 SNAT for outgoing (optional) |

### SECURITY & AUTHENTICATION:

| Variable | Default | Notes |
|----------|---------|-------|
| MAILCOW_PASS_SCHEME | BLF-CRYPT | Password hash algorithm |
| DOVECOT_MASTER_USER | (empty) | Dovecot master user (auto-gen if empty) |
| DOVECOT_MASTER_PASS | (empty) | Dovecot master pass (auto-gen if empty) |
| WEBAUTHN_ONLY_TRUSTED_VENDORS | n | Restrict WebAuthn to trusted vendors |
| ACL_ANYONE | disallow | Enable "anyone" pseudo-user ACL |
| ALLOW_ADMIN_EMAIL_LOGIN | n | Allow admins to login as email users in SOGo |

### SOGO (WEBMAIL/CALENDAR/CONTACTS):

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| SOGO_EXPIRE_SESSION | 480 | Session timeout in minutes |
| SOGO_URL_ENCRYPTION_KEY | (auto-gen 16 chars) | URL encryption key for SOGo |
| SKIP_SOGO | n | Disable SOGo (webmail, DAV, ActiveSync) |

### ADVANCED FILTERING & SCANNING:

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| SKIP_CLAMD | n | Disable ClamAV anti-virus |
| SKIP_OLEFY | n | Disable Olefy (Office doc scanning) |
| SKIP_FTS | n | Disable full-text search (Dovecot indexing) |
| SKIP_RSPAMD | n | Disable Rspamd spam filtering |
| FTS_HEAP | 128 | FTS heap size in MB |
| FTS_PROCS | 1 | Max FTS indexing processes |
| MAILDIR_GC_TIME | 7200 | Garbage collection time (minutes) |
| MAILDIR_SUB | Maildir | Maildir subdirectory name |
| SPAMHAUS_DQS_KEY | (empty) | Spamhaus DQS key (if on blocked ASN) |

### WATCHDOG MONITORING:

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| USE_WATCHDOG | y | Enable container health monitoring |
| WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_EMAIL | (empty) | Email addresses for alerts |
| WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_WEBHOOK | (empty) | Webhook URL for notifications |
| WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_WEBHOOK_BODY | (empty) | Custom webhook JSON body |
| WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_BAN | n | Notify on IP bans |
| WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_START | y | Notify when watchdog starts |
| WATCHDOG_EXTERNAL_CHECKS | n | Enable external relay checks |
| WATCHDOG_VERBOSE | n | Enable verbose logging |
| LOG_LINES | 9999 | Max log lines in Redis |

### API CONFIGURATION:

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| API_KEY | (empty) | Read-write API key |
| API_KEY_READ_ONLY | (empty) | Read-only API key |
| API_ALLOW_FROM | invalid | Comma-separated IPs allowed for API |

### MISC:

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| DOCKER_COMPOSE_VERSION | (auto-detected) | Docker Compose version (native/standalone) |
| ENABLE_IPV6 | true | Enable IPv6 support |
| SKIP_UNBOUND_HEALTHCHECK | n | Skip Unbound DNS health checks |
| DISABLE_NETFILTER_ISOLATION_RULE | n | Isolate Docker network |

---

### SETUP MECHANISM

The configuration uses a **`.env` symlink** that points to `mailcow.conf`:

```bash
ln -s mailcow.conf .env
```

This allows Docker Compose to automatically load all variables from `mailcow.conf` into the environment.

### MINIMAL WORKING CONFIGURATION FOR mail.lgucalapan.ph

To get mailcow running for this domain, you need to create `mailcow.conf` with at minimum:

```bash
MAILCOW_HOSTNAME=mail.lgucalapan.ph
MAILCOW_TZ=Asia/Manila
MAILCOW_PASS_SCHEME=BLF-CRYPT
DBNAME=mailcow
DBUSER=mailcow
DBPASS=[random 28+ alphanumeric chars]
DBROOT=[random 28+ alphanumeric chars]
REDISPASS=[random 28+ alphanumeric chars]
HTTP_PORT=80
HTTPS_PORT=443
HTTP_REDIRECT=y
SMTP_PORT=25
SMTPS_PORT=465
SUBMISSION_PORT=587
IMAP_PORT=143
IMAPS_PORT=993
TZ=Asia/Manila
COMPOSE_PROJECT_NAME=mailcowdockerized
ACL_ANYONE=disallow
MAILDIR_GC_TIME=7200
ADDITIONAL_SAN=
AUTODISCOVER_SAN=y
ADDITIONAL_SERVER_NAMES=
SKIP_LETS_ENCRYPT=n
ACME_DNS_CHALLENGE=n
ACME_ACCOUNT_EMAIL=admin@mail.lgucalapan.ph
ENABLE_SSL_SNI=n
SKIP_IP_CHECK=n
SKIP_HTTP_VERIFICATION=n
SKIP_CLAMD=n
SKIP_OLEFY=n
SKIP_SOGO=n
SKIP_FTS=n
FTS_HEAP=128
FTS_PROCS=1
ALLOW_ADMIN_EMAIL_LOGIN=n
USE_WATCHDOG=y
WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_BAN=n
WATCHDOG_NOTIFY_START=y
WATCHDOG_EXTERNAL_CHECKS=n
WATCHDOG_VERBOSE=n
LOG_LINES=9999
IPV4_NETWORK=172.22.1
IPV6_NETWORK=fd4d:6169:6c63:6f77::/64
SOGO_EXPIRE_SESSION=480
SOGO_URL_ENCRYPTION_KEY=[random 16 alphanumeric]
DOVECOT_MASTER_USER=
DOVECOT_MASTER_PASS=
WEBAUTHN_ONLY_TRUSTED_VENDORS=n
SPAMHAUS_DQS_KEY=
ENABLE_IPV6=true
DISABLE_NETFILTER_ISOLATION_RULE=n
```

The `generate_config.sh` script automates the creation of this file interactively, generating random passwords and keys automatically.

# Komodo: Overview & Deployment

## What is Komodo?

Komodo is a web-based platform for infrastructure management. Per the documentation, it enables users to "monitor servers, builds, deployments, and automated procedures." The system comprises two components: **Core** (web server with API and UI) and **Periphery** (stateless agents on connected servers).

## Container Deployment

### GitHub Repository Integration
The documentation states Komodo can "Define compose files in the UI, on the host, or in a git repo with auto-deploy on push." However, the source material doesn't provide specific technical details about the pull mechanism or configuration steps.

### Orchestration Approach
Komodo uses **Docker Compose** for stack deployments. The documentation mentions "Deploy compose stacks" as a distinct capability, separate from individual container management. It also supports "Docker Swarms" for cluster-level deployments, but doesn't describe proprietary orchestration beyond these standards.

### Stack Definition
A "Stack" appears to be a collection of services defined via Docker Compose files that can be deployed together across your infrastructure.

## API & Automation

Komodo exposes "a REST and WebSocket API for programmatic access," with client libraries available in:
- Komodo CLI
- Rust crate
- NPM package
- curl examples

## Configuration Management

Komodo provides "shared variable and secret with interpolation across all resources," enabling centralized management of environment configuration and secrets across deployments.

**Note:** Specific API endpoints, git configuration workflows, and detailed deployment procedures aren't covered in this introductory documentation.

```text
# Komodo Resource Types Overview

## Key Resources

**Stack**: "Deploy with docker compose." You can provide the compose file through the UI or store it in a git repo with webhook-triggered auto-redeploy on code push. Multiple compose files can be combined, and environment variables are supported.

**Deployment**: Handles container deployment on a connected Server. It enables service management at the container level, with orchestration through Procedures and ResourceSyncs.

**Build**: Converts application source code into Docker images and pushes them to a registry. The source must be a git repository containing a Dockerfile.

**Repo**: "Put scripts in git repos, and run them on a Server, or using a Builder." This enables automation, binary compilation, and other scripted tasks.

## Git Integration

Git repositories are central to Komodo's workflow. Repos can contain compose files (for auto-deployment via webhooks), Dockerfiles (for image building), or scripts for automation tasks. The system includes credential management for accessing private repositories, allowing resources to authenticate with git services and Docker registries automatically.

## Resource Architecture

All resources share common properties: unique names and IDs within their type, plus taggable metadata. Many resources depend on git access and benefit from Komodo's integrated token management system for secure credential handling.
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
# Komodo Core Setup Overview

Komodo is a server management platform requiring Docker for deployment. The setup documentation covers multiple configuration approaches:

**Core Requirements:**
You'll need Docker installed first, as stated: "To run Komodo, you will need Docker."

**Database Options:**
Two primary deployment paths are available:

1. **MongoDB** - The preferred choice for most installations, used for storing "all resource configuration, user accounts, audit logs, and system state"

2. **FerretDB** - A Postgres-based alternative recommended for systems incompatible with recent MongoDB versions

**Setup Pathways:**
The documentation provides guides for:
- Docker Compose deployments with either database
- Advanced configuration including custom certificates and OAuth/OIDC providers
- Adding additional managed servers via the Periphery agent
- Automated backup and restore capabilities with "gzip-compressed" storage and "the most recent 14 backups" retained by default

The setup process is designed to be modular, allowing you to start with a core installation and progressively expand your managed infrastructure as needed.
```

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Komodo (komo.do):

## Overview

Komodo is a platform for deploying and managing containers and compose stacks across all your servers, allowing you to view status and logs, connect to container shells, and orchestrate with automations.

## Docker Compose Deployment

Komodo can deploy compose stacks, with compose files that can be defined in the UI, on the host, or in a git repo with auto-deploy on push. More specifically, Komodo clones git repos onto the host to deploy, with changes tracked in git and support for webhooks to auto-redeploy on push.

## Configuration Management

Komodo allows you to orchestrate all configuration declaratively by defining it in TOML files, which are checked into a git repo. Komodo is able to create, update, delete, and deploy resources declared in TOML files by diffing them against existing resources, with files that can be configured in UI, in a local file, or in a git repo, and the Komodo Core backend will poll the files for updates and alert about pending changes.

## Setup Requirements

To run Komodo, you will need Docker. Additionally, every server you want to manage with Komodo needs the Periphery agent installed.

The documentation is available at komo.do/docs with sections covering setup, deployment, builds, swarms, and resources.

# Docker Compose Stack Deployment in Komodo

Based on the documentation provided, here's what's available:

## 1. Creating a Stack from Git

Komodo supports git-based deployment through these configuration fields:
- `git_provider`: Git provider domain (defaults to `github.com`)
- `git_account`: Account name for private repositories
- `repo`: Repository in `owner/repo` format
- `branch`: Branch to clone (defaults to `main`)

The documentation states: *"Komodo clones the repo onto the host to deploy. Changes are tracked in git and you can use webhooks to auto-redeploy on push."*

## 2. Compose File Handling

Three methods are supported:
- Write files directly in the UI (Komodo writes to host at deploy time)
- Reference existing files on the server via `file_paths`
- Clone from git repository

The `file_paths` field accepts *"a list of compose files. Supports composing multiple files via `docker compose -f ... -f ...`"*

## 3. Environment Variables

The `environment` field accepts *"environment variables written to a `.env` file and passed via `--env-file`."* It also supports variable interpolation.

## 4. Webhooks for Auto-Deploy

The documentation mentions webhooks exist for auto-redeployment on git push but provides no configuration details beyond referencing the webhooks documentation page.

## 5. Git Configuration

Limited details provided:
- `git_provider`, `git_account`, `repo`, and `branch` fields configure the source
- No authentication mechanism details are documented

## 6. File Paths

The `file_paths` configuration supports multiple compose files for composition.

## 7-8. API & Clone Details

**Not covered** in this documentation. The API and repository cloning mechanisms aren't detailed in the provided content.

I found relevant information about Komodo (komo.do) webhooks, auto-deployment, git integration, and environment variables. Here's what the documentation shows:

## Webhooks and Auto-Deploy on Git Push

To set up webhooks, go to your git provider's repo Settings > Webhooks and create a new webhook with the Payload URL set to the copied URL, Content-type set to application/json, and Secret set to your KOMODO_WEBHOOK_SECRET. Push events should be selected as the trigger.

Your git provider sends webhooks on every push to any branch, but the Build, Repo, etc. only cares about a specific branch, so the webhook will trigger the action only on pushes to the branch configured on the resource.

## Stack Deployment

Changes are tracked in git and you can use webhooks to auto-redeploy on push. Komodo supports defining compose files in the UI, on the host, or in a git repo with auto-deploy on push.

## Environment Variables

Komodo enables you to easily manage environment variables passed to containers through the environment tab of the configuration on the deployment page. Komodo supports shared variables and secrets with interpolation across all resources.

## Webhook Configuration Options

Builds only have the /build action, Repos can select between /pull, /clone, or /build, and Stacks have /deploy and /refresh actions.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

<summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://komo.do/docs/automate/webhooks`

```text
# Komodo Webhooks: Complete Guide

## Webhook URL Format

Komodo webhooks follow this structure:
```
https://<HOST>/listener/<AUTH_TYPE>/<RESOURCE_TYPE>/<ID_OR_NAME>/<EXECUTION>
```

Key components:
- **HOST**: Your Komodo endpoint (use a public proxy if on a private network)
- **AUTH_TYPE**: `github` or `gitlab` (also supports Gitea and Forgejo)
- **RESOURCE_TYPE**: `build`, `repo`, `stack`, `sync`, `procedure`, or `action`
- **ID_OR_NAME**: Resource identifier (ID recommended if names change)
- **EXECUTION**: Varies by resource type

## Stack Actions

For Stack resources, available executions are:
- `/deploy` — triggers stack deployment
- `/refresh` — refreshes stack configuration

## Setup Instructions

1. Locate the webhook URL on your resource's Config page in Komodo
2. In your git provider's repo settings, navigate to Webhooks
3. Create a new webhook with:
   - **Payload URL**: Your copied Komodo webhook URL
   - **Content-type**: `application/json`
   - **Secret**: Your `KOMODO_WEBHOOK_SECRET` environment variable
   - **Trigger**: Push events

## Branch Filtering

"Your git provider sends webhooks on pushes to any branch. Komodo only triggers the action when the push matches the branch configured on the resource." This means a Stack pointing to the `main` branch will ignore pushes to other branches automatically.

## Auto-Deploy Setup

To enable auto-deploy on git push:
1. Use the `/deploy` execution in your Stack's webhook URL
2. Configure the webhook to trigger on push events
3. Komodo will automatically deploy only when changes reach your configured branch
```
</details></tool-use>

247	# Normally this should be untouched but if you decided to use either of those you can switch it manually here.
248	# Please be aware that at least one of those variants should be installed on your machine or mailcow will fail.
249	DOCKER_COMPOSE_VERSION=${COMPOSE_VERSION}

251	# Set this to "allow" to enable the anyone pseudo user. Disabled by default.
252	# When enabled, ACL can be created, that apply to "All authenticated users"
253	# This should probably only be activated on mail hosts, that are used exclusively by one organisation.
254	# Otherwise a user might share data with too many other users.
255	ACL_ANYONE=disallow
256

258	# Deleted domains and mailboxes are moved to /var/vmail/_garbage/timestamp_sanitizedstring
259	# How long should objects remain in the garbage until they are being deleted? (value in minutes)
260	# Check interval is hourly
261	MAILDIR_GC_TIME=7200
262	
263	# Additional SAN for the certificate
264	#
265	# You can use wildcard records to create specific names for every domain you add to mailcow.
266	# Example: Add domains "example.com" and "example.net" to mailcow, change ADDITIONAL_SAN to a value like:
267	#ADDITIONAL_SAN=imap.*,smtp.*
268	# This will expand the certificate to "imap.example.com", "smtp.example.com", "imap.example.net", "smtp.example.net"
269	# plus every domain you add in the future.
270	#
271	# You can also just add static names...

273	# ...or combine wildcard and static names:
274	#ADDITIONAL_SAN=imap.*,srv1.example.com

278	# This can be useful to switch off in case you are in a scenario where a reverse proxy already handles those.
279	# There are mixed scenarios where ports 80,443 are occupied and you do not want to share certs
280	# between services. So acme-mailcow obtains for maildomains and all web-things get handled
281	# in the reverse proxy.

288	# If the server name does not match a known site, Nginx decides by best-guess and may redirect users to the wrong web root.
289	# You can understand this as server_name directive in Nginx.

291	ADDITIONAL_SERVER_NAMES=
292	
293	# Skip running ACME (acme-mailcow, Let's Encrypt certs) - y/n

```sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Komodo-compatible setup script for mailcow
# This script initializes mailcow.conf from the template with generated secrets.
# Run once on initial deployment. Subsequent deploys (via Komodo webhook) only
# need `docker compose up -d` which Komodo handles automatically.

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]:-$0}")" >/dev/null 2>&1 && pwd)"
cd "$SCRIPT_DIR"

echo "=== mailcow Komodo Setup for mail.lgucalapan.ph ==="

# Check if mailcow.conf already exists
if [ -f mailcow.conf ]; then
  echo "mailcow.conf already exists."
  read -r -p "Overwrite? This will back up the current config. [y/N] " response
  case $response in
    [yY][eE][sS]|[yY])
      cp mailcow.conf "mailcow.conf.bak.$(date +%s)"
      echo "Backed up existing config."
      ;;
    *)
      echo "Keeping existing config. Exiting."
      exit 0
      ;;
  esac
fi

# Generate random secrets
gen_secret() {
  LC_ALL=C </dev/urandom tr -dc A-Za-z0-9 2>/dev/null | head -c "${1:-28}"
}

DBPASS=$(gen_secret 28)
DBROOT=$(gen_secret 28)
REDISPASS=$(gen_secret 28)
SOGO_KEY=$(gen_secret 16)

echo "Generating mailcow.conf from template..."

sed \
  -e "s/__DBPASS__/${DBPASS}/" \
  -e "s/__DBROOT__/${DBROOT}/" \
  -e "s/__REDISPASS__/${REDISPASS}/" \
  -e "s/__SOGO_KEY__/${SOGO_KEY}/" \
  mailcow.conf.template > mailcow.conf

chmod 600 mailcow.conf

# Create .env symlink if not present
if [ ! -L .env ]; then
  ln -sf mailcow.conf .env
  echo "Created .env -> mailcow.conf symlink."
fi

# Create required directories
mkdir -p data/assets/ssl

# Generate snake-oil certificate if no cert exists
if [ ! -f data/assets/ssl/cert.pem ]; then
  echo "Generating snake-oil SSL certificate..."
  openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:4096 \
    -keyout data/assets/ssl/key.pem \
    -out data/assets/ssl/cert.pem \
    -days 365 \
    -subj "/C=PH/ST=Calapan/L=Calapan/O=lgucalapan/OU=mail/CN=mail.lgucalapan.ph" \
    -sha256 -nodes 2>/dev/null
  echo "Snake-oil certificate generated. Let's Encrypt will replace it automatically."
fi

# Create rspamd password placeholder if missing
[ ! -f ./data/conf/rspamd/override.d/worker-controller-password.inc ] && \
  echo '# Placeholder' > ./data/conf/rspamd/override.d/worker-controller-password.inc

# Set app_info
mailcow_git_version=$(git describe --tags "$(git rev-list --tags --max-count=1)" 2>/dev/null || git rev-parse --short HEAD)
mailcow_git_commit=$(git rev-parse HEAD 2>/dev/null || echo "unknown")
mailcow_git_commit_date=$(git log -1 --format=%ci 2>/dev/null || echo "unknown")
git_branch=$(git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD 2>/dev/null || echo "master")

cat > data/web/inc/app_info.inc.php << APPEOF
<?php
  \$MAILCOW_GIT_VERSION="${mailcow_git_version}";
  \$MAILCOW_LAST_GIT_VERSION="";
  \$MAILCOW_GIT_OWNER="bonitobonita24";
  \$MAILCOW_GIT_REPO="mail_lgucalapan_ph";
  \$MAILCOW_GIT_URL="https://github.com/bonitobonita24/mail_lgucalapan_ph";
  \$MAILCOW_GIT_COMMIT="${mailcow_git_commit}";
  \$MAILCOW_GIT_COMMIT_DATE="${mailcow_git_commit_date}";
  \$MAILCOW_BRANCH="${git_branch}";
  \$MAILCOW_UPDATEDAT=$(date +%s);
?>
APPEOF

echo ""
echo "=== Setup Complete ==="
echo ""
echo "  Hostname: mail.lgucalapan.ph"
echo "  Timezone: Asia/Manila"
echo "  Config:   mailcow.conf (chmod 600)"
echo ""
echo "Next steps:"
echo "  1. docker compose pull"
echo "  2. docker compose up -d"
echo "  3. Access https://mail.lgucalapan.ph"
echo "  4. Login: admin / moohoo (change immediately!)"
echo ""
echo "DNS records required:"
echo "  A/AAAA  mail.lgucalapan.ph          -> your server IP"
echo "  MX      lgucalapan.ph               -> mail.lgucalapan.ph (priority 10)"
echo "  TXT     lgucalapan.ph               -> v=spf1 mx a -all"
echo "  TXT     _dmarc.lgucalapan.ph        -> v=DMARC1; p=reject; rua=mailto:admin@lgucalapan.ph"
echo "  TXT     dkim._domainkey.lgucalapan.ph -> (get from mailcow admin UI after first start)"
echo "  CNAME   autodiscover.lgucalapan.ph  -> mail.lgucalapan.ph"
echo "  CNAME   autoconfig.lgucalapan.ph    -> mail.lgucalapan.ph"
echo "  SRV     _autodiscover._tcp.lgucalapan.ph -> 0 1 443 mail.lgucalapan.ph"

```

Here's a summary of everything set up:

## What was created

| File | Tracked in Git? | Purpose |

| `CREDENTIALS.md` | No (gitignored) | Komodo credentials + full setup instructions |

## Deployment Flow

```
You (local) --> git push origin master --> GitHub
                                            |
                              Webhook fires |
                                            v
                                    Komodo (kmd.lgucalapan.ph)
                                            |
                              pulls repo    |
                                            v
                                    docker compose up -d
                                    (on target server)
```

## To complete the setup, you need to:

1. **In Komodo UI** (https://kmd.lgucalapan.ph/) — Create a Stack pointing to `bonitobonita24/mail_lgucalapan_ph` on branch `master`
2. **Set up GitHub webhook** — Copy the webhook URL from Komodo and add it to GitHub repo Settings > Webhooks
3. **SSH to server once** — Run `./komodo-setup.sh` to generate `mailcow.conf` with random secrets
4. **Configure DNS** — Point `mail.lgucalapan.ph` A record to your server IP, plus MX, SPF, DMARC, and DKIM records
